extra_fields.Why Interested:
1. I would love to share knowledge and experience with current students, helping them to succeed in their careers.

2. By staying involved in the ECE community, I can stay up-to-date with the latest developments in the field and continue to be a part of it.

3. Apart from that I can develop leadership and coaching skills, and gain satisfaction from helping others succeed.

records.about_yourself: A little bit about myself. I was born and raised in Mumbai, India and received my master’s degree in Computer Engineering from North Carolina State University. I have always been passionate about working in silicon development. Outside work I enjoy hiking, playing pool, playing badminton and table tennis. I’m a big fan of reading books (would welcome book recommendations) and try to stay active and engaged in the community.

extra_fields.Why Interested: Throughout my career, I was (and I am) always inspired by my mentors, and I fundamentally believe good mentoring will pave way for extraordinary pathways. As I am making my way through the career ladder, I would love to mentor my juniors at NC State.

extra_fields.Previous Involvement: A couple years ago, a sophomore in the ECE program (Caden Tolentino) connected with me on LinkedIn and asked to chat. He was looking for advice on selecting which concentration to select for his Electrical Engineering degree. We spoke over the phone for about an hour while I asked him what his favorite classes so far had been, what kind of work he was looking to do upon graduation, and just got to know him. He's on the Men's Soccer team and I'm an avid soccer fan/player, so we had much in common. We've kept in touch and met up as recently as last week, he's starting his senior year so I was inquiring about his senior design project. I've enjoyed loosely mentoring him as I've had, and would love to do something like this in the future with someone else!

records.about_yourself:
I've been married to my wife Ainsley, whom I meet in 2018 when I was at NCSU and she at Meredith, since 2021. We live a minute off of Buck Jones Rd., within walking distance of the apartment I lived in when attending NCSU in 2019, so you could say we love the area! We have a cat named Winona, whom Ainsley adopted about a week before we started dating.
I enjoy keeping in shape and playing soccer, I play for a men's recreational team on the weekends and for a coed team in Fuquay Varina on Mondays/Wednesdays. The coed league is special for me because my Dad is the goalie for our team, it's extremely cool to be able to play soccer in a competitive environment with him after having been coached by him growing up. I love board games, and I'm also part of a book club with my local friends from high school - so far we've read Brothers Karamazov, Hyperion (my pick), Frankenstein, Mother Night, and Moby Dick (very much not my pick).
records.professional_experience:
I've worked for a local controls integrator called Industrial Automated Systems since graduating in December 2019. Controls was the selected concentration for my Electrical Engineering degree, and I had connections to the company that procured me an internship for a few months before graduating, plus I had previous experience interning for various controls integration companies.
At IAS, I focused on SCADA development at Cree/Wolfspeed, Durham. Working with SCADA as opposed to simply PLC/HMI development put me in a position to work with all members of the integration project scope, and being one of the sole SCADA (AVEVA/Wonderware) developers for IAS at Wolfspeed gave me plenty of work to keep me busy with all of the asynchronous projects we had going on.
I have left IAS, the only company I've ever worked at full time, for Sipe Solutions (an even smaller integration company) to fill the need for a role at Fujifilm in Holly Springs as a Building Management System/Validated Data System Automation Support Engineer. I have primarily been assisting with the troubleshooting of long standing systemic problems their AVEVA systems have been faced with in getting this plant up and running since 2022.

extra_fields.Previous Involvement: I’ve been a mentee in the mentor program previously.
extra_fields.Why Interested: I’ve always enjoyed mentoring and helping others to the best of my ability. Becoming an ECE Alumni Mentor is a way for me to give back to the department that has played such a pivotal role in shaping who I am today, both personally and professionally.

records.about_yourself: I’m an outdoor enthusiast who loves traveling—I’ve visited around 10 states in the US over the past two years. This year, I’ve been on a couple of hikes and plan to do many more. I enjoy staying active, from hitting the gym regularly to playing sports. I recently started tennis lessons and also love playing pickleball. I’m also a big foodie who loves exploring different cuisines wherever I go.
records.professional_experience: I completed my undergraduate degree in 2018 and began my career at Texas Instruments as an Analog Layout Engineer. Early on, I realized that my true interests lay elsewhere, which led me to pursue a Master’s in Computer Engineering at NC State University in Fall 2023, specializing in digital design and computer architecture. During my master’s program, I gained diverse industry experience through internships—first as a Modem Design Intern at Qualcomm in the summer, and later as a RISC-V CPU RTL Intern at Tenstorrent in the spring. After graduating in May 2025, I rejoined Texas Instruments, this time in a role aligned with my passion, as a Digital Design Engineer.

• Drive cross-functional collaboration with Sales, Project Management, R&D, Factory Ops, and Field Services to ensure technical excellence, schedule alignment, and process scalability.
• Champion team development through mentorship, technical upskilling, and defined role progression frameworks (IC → SME → TL).
• Partner with senior leadership on engineering org design, and front-end engagement strategies to increase vertical responsiveness (Utility, I&C, DC&S).
• Standardized engineering quality gates across project lifecycle (design approval and manufacturing release), reducing rework through proactive SME engagement and peer QA review.
• Serve as escalation point for critical design risks, field issues, or major quality concerns; lead resolution plans with factory and engineering heads.

records.professional_experience: I've worked in ASIC verification and validation for the past 24 years, with experience ranging from pre-silicon design verification on designs up to 80 Billion Transistors to post-silicon validation and design test in a lab-based environment. I'm currently a senior ASIC verification engineer for NVIDIA, where I've worked since 2019. Prior to that, I have worked at Intel, AMD, Hewlett-Packard (now Hewlett-Packard Enterprise), Compaq, and Lockheed Martin. I've worked in several regions around the country, from New Jersey to Houston to Austin to Portland and now back to the Raleigh-Durham area.

extra_fields.Previous Involvement:
I've worked with undergraduate students at both Duke and NCSU mentoring them in their own specific research projects (2 students while at NCSU so far 2 at Duke). 

When I was in my senior year of undergrad, I was part of a mentorship program with Caterpillar but it fell through when I decided on graduate school. 

I did a PhD and postdoc which had apprenticeship-like mentorship components by my advisors.
extra_fields.Why Interested: My postdoc advisor (Dr. G Allan Johnson) was especially helpful during the time I was figuring out what I was going to do with my PhD after school and the whole training to become more statically minded. I've really enjoyed the opportunity to work with students both at Duke and NCSU.  I would love to be able to help a student in a similar way that is not directly working with them for research tasks (which was mainly what I had done in the past).

My immediate family (mom, dad -- retired telecommunications engineer, and older brother -- network engineer for banking corporation) live nearby. My dad and brother were both also trained as electrical engineers. Extended family is mainly from rural southwest Michigan.

records.degrees_text: BS EE 2013, MS EE 2016, PhD EE 2019
records.professional_experience:
I was really uncertain what I major I wanted when I first started in undergraduate for engineering. Engineering wise, I was between biomedical/electrical/civil as possible options when I applied. I started during the Great Recession which made the idea of doing something related to the housing industry scary. I doubted at the time I would be able to get into the biomedical program with how exclusive it was and thought electrical while it didn't have biology would cover similar areas with more generality. Initially after a convocation major information event, I wanted to do electrical engineering focused in power because the renewables concentration was very new and I liked environmental causes. But once I took the power class, it was not of interest.  

I found the E&M courses in undergrad. I did research in my undergraduate in an optics lab (ie E&M you can see) for Dr. Escuti (no longer at NC State now at Meta) who later became my graduate advisor. I worked for years as a graduate student making polarization optics components (color filters/lenses etc with liquid crystals) for various collaborators at NC State (like Dr. Kudenov), within the US, and abroad who had specialized imaging requirements.

After my PhD,  I was burned out and didn't think I could move to the west coast (where the majority of jobs for optics seemed to be at the time) for a variety of personal reasons. I applied to an imaging postdoc at Duke (as I've always been into biomedical or human centered imaging tasks) who passed my resume on to my current boss who does pre-clinical super high resolution MRI and compares it to light sheet microscopy results in an ex-vivo mouse model. He needed someone to do the statistical analysis of the MRI contrasts obtained, so I retrained to learn about statistical approaches and techniques. 

Rather than do the typical postdoc path towards academia (and leading my own lab), I've taken an approach to become a staff researcher. I became full-time staff at Duke University Medical Center in December 2021. I get to do day to day science figuring out how the brains of these mice are effected by the various diseases, and just process of aging, that they are subjected to which hopefully can inform next-generation clinical investigations.

extra_fields.Previous Involvement: I was a mentee
extra_fields.Why Interested: When I was approaching graduation, I was so full of nerves. If I could provide any insight into the transition from academia into the workforce, I would love to help however I can. I would especially love to help any rising women in STEM who are looking for insight into working in a male dominate workplace.

Outside of my academic and profession pursuits, I have many hobbies such as Sailing, Scuba Diving, Golfing, and Woodworking.

Outside of my Hobbies I am a big fan of Ice Hockey(Which I played growing up) and love to watch the hurricanes. I also love to watch the Wolfpack on Saturdays in the fall and always try to make it to at least one home football game a year.  When I get the chance, I love to travel; my trips to Italy and Alaska have been some of my favorite. I also love food weather thats cooking a great meal or trying a new restaurant in the city.
records.degrees_text: BS CPE 2020, BS EE 2020, MS CNE 2022
records.professional_experience:
Overall, my professional interests lie in Software engineer, Software and Network Security , Cloud Technologies, and Agile Project Management.

Formally Trained as a software engineer focusing on how software is used in networking and security applications in my time at NC State. While at NC State I held 3 internships as both a Graduate and Undergraduate student. One was an embedded/software testing internship with Plexus working on a robotical surgical system. The other two were with NetApp devloping tools such as a language parser to help diagnose problems withing cloud systems. Also while at NC state I was a Makerspace Student Ambassador where I worked to maintain the libraries fleet of 3D printers, Laser Cutter, and Raspberry Pi's. In the makerspace I also consulted with students faculty and staff around campus to provide them with the engineering knowledge they needed to complete their research or projects. 

At my current role At Lockheed martin I have worked on two teams. The first, created a software microservice biased for a Nation State secure satellite communication system. I was in charge of developing backend rest endpoints. As well as doing security sys admin work to configure firewalls and other network security measure across our servers. The second project I worked on is with the Human spaceflight devison working on software tools to build the NASA Class A flight software for the ORION capsule used in the Artemis lunar missions. On this project I audit and verify code for defects in flight software as well as devlope full stack solutions that allow other engineers to more easily meet requirements.

Besides work, I was inducted into NC State's Beta Eta chapter of IEEE Eta Kappa Nu in 2018. I Have been an active member of the organization since then, serving first as chapter advisor then as chapter president. Now as an alumni I serve as the chapter advisor and still mentor chapter leaders to this day.

extra_fields.Previous Involvement: I multiple mentorship experiences from my time in the US Army to my current role as a senior engineer working with several new grad engineers in our company. I have also worked with college interns almost every year over the past 10 years.
extra_fields.Why Interested: I enjoyed the impact I made as a graduate student TA in undergrad classes and continue to enjoy interacting with and supporting up and coming students and engineers.

records.about_yourself: Dancing & reading
records.professional_experience: RF and Wireless Communications

extra_fields.Previous Involvement:
I've been a mentee during all of my jobs so far. My first mentor in civil engineering helped me discover that I didn't like working in civil engineering and to appreciate the monolith of government regulations in buildings and medical fields. When I first moved into college, I met my second professional mentor, an old friend from high school who was the news editor at the NC State technician. He encouraged me to try journalism and helped me interview all sorts of people, writing about critical issues like the 2016 election, housing inequality at NC State, tuition and fees at NC State, and racial/accessibility issues on campus in 2016 and 2017. He made me feel important in the news room and made excellent software systems for reading in news ideas. After the newspaper, I was a mentee at my summer internship at Plexus corp. My mentor was an entry-level software engineer that had recently joined the company. He helped give me excellent feedback on my project ideas and helped guide me toward literature to help me learn more about production-scale software development philosophies. I still have a mentor from Plexus who lives in Colorado who was the senior engineer and SCRUM master for the robotic surgeon testing suite I developed there. He gives me career opportunities when he finds them, gives me feedback on my work in Raleigh, and we enjoy playing board games together. 

At NC State, my coursework and research led to at least a dozen mentee opportunities (I cannot write about them all): I was first a mentee in ECE 200 and ECE 309, the car class under my TAs John and Brody. Brody gave me excellent feedback on my programming approach and helped guide me toward becoming an intern at Plexus. He also helped explain the intricacies of FDA clearance and approval so that I could be a better intern there. John helped me appreciate my problem solving fluency in mathematics and to pursue higher-level mathematics in control theory and analysis at NC State. I really enjoyed talking through physics problems with him. I was an entrepreneurial mentor and mentee in the NC State entrepreneurship program, receiving project advice on a robotic stove knob from Marshall Brain II. Marshall gave my team good feedback on the practicality of our inventions and helped connect us to manufacturing methods and design concerns, even when we felt completely lost. In my research, I was mentored by Dr. Bitzer, the inventor of the plasma screen. He shared his love for intuitive problem solving with me and helped me feel appreciated and confident working with extremely complex patents and unused software, even when I didn't know the way forward and needed room to grow. Lastly, I was mentored by Dr. Kolbas at NC State during his ECE 303 class and afterward when I made a make-a-thon event for my department. Dr. Kolbas directed my passion for helping other students learn invention skills and helped me to become more humble and curious around simple physics problems. I remember him telling me that it didn't matter what math I would need to learn in the future if I weren't curious now about simple things, like ice, clouds, arcs, and real-world discrepancies compared to my models.

I've been a mentor to college students in two different ways: as a TA in ECE 200, and as the creator of a one-time make-a-thon for engineering students. I was a TA on 3 separate occasions in my 3.5 undergrad years at NC State, and my philosophy was that student names and listening were far more important than solely delivering course content. From our first classes, I made an effort to greet every single student and to ensure that they knew they could talk to me about anything. Some students needed help navigating the ECE program's career outcomes and internships while others needed help understanding mathematical concepts. I hope that in my time talking with them, I was able to help them become more successful and to contribute to positive engineering outcomes -- some of them still run into me at the grocery store, and some of them have been published by NC State's engineering magazine for their inventions. In my make-a-thon, I was mentored by Elizabeth in the ECE undergrad office. She helped me understand how to organize my project to be successful and helped me understand my aversions to deadlines and feedback better. She helped me become more vulnerable and to host 3 electrical engineering projects and a contest day for other students using funds from Dr. Kolbas. I mentored 3 project groups since I was unable to find any willing ECE student mentors: I worked with the groups to develop their social media presence, development process, and to feel comfortable with failure when some of their inventions were more difficult or complicated than planned.

Lastly, I've been a mentor to younger children in four ways: a tutoring program, a literacy program, a soldering skills program, and as a member of the radio club at NC State. I was a tutor to Sudanese immigrants in Greensboro, NC for 6 years while I was in middle and high school. I helped provide them with free after-school math tutoring and out-loud reading -- the reading part being especially difficult. In high school, I was a mentor in a program called Brooks Buddies where I worked with a child for 4 years weekly on Tuesdays or Thursdays -- I'd listen and learn from him talking about life and his family, teach him about my experiences, and read books with him or talk about books that we were reading to help him develop his literacy skills and love for reading. I was a mentor in Girls Engineering Change to elementary, middle, and high school girls. I discussed careers in electrical engineering with them, drew simplified circuit diagrams with them, and made them feel comfortable working with electronics and soldering. When I was in the radio club, I helped host a Boy Scout radio merit badge day where I taught antenna physics and radio uses to troops around the triangle area. I got to use my homemade yogi antenna with them and to demystify electrodynamics for a young audience.
extra_fields.Why Interested: I'm interested in helping make a community of mentorship for ECE students so that they feel less alone and more supported in this job market. It's really difficult starting out in a remote job for me, and I think that connecting with students will give them the skills and support they need to make amazing contributions in whatever they do.

records.about_yourself:
I play piano almost daily, Rachmaninoff, Scriabin, religious music, and Debussy are current interests. I perform in worship services and at public pianos when I can find them, and in the next year, I may join a local group to perform with. I've played for 16 years now. I also play guitar and trombone but haven't performed with them since the pandemic.

I love to ride my bike and run around the Kentwood neighborhood and the Raleigh Greenway. I also do indoor FTP training when the weather is bad. I do calisthenics, hiking, walking, and backpacking as well.

I'm a big board game nerd: I host a board game night with my friends weekly and really enjoy complex, thematic strategy games like Spirit Island and nuanced classic card games like karma and canasta.

I love traveling to historical and natural areas. During the first months of the pandemic, I explored all of the county parks in Wake County. My recent trips include the Joyce Kilmer memorial forest, the Grayson Highlands, Fort Fisher, and Monticello.

Sometimes, when I find a new codebase, I may open it up and modify it. I recently discovered an abandoned Android application that selectively disables a pixel array on the AMOLED screen on my Google Pixel phone.
records.professional_experience:
My fields of interest are analysis, statistics, machine learning, control theory, electrodynamics, circuit design, sensing, embedded software, networking, and advanced computing. My main interest is improving the utility of existing technology and helping technology adapt to environmental and medical applications. 

I'm currently an embedded engineer for Signalscape, Inc. developing our Android software portfolio. I created a training site for new engineers, our first successful Android application, and am working on a release for our next Android application next year. I've also integrated a cellphone protocol into an existing embedded device so that it could make phone calls. My favorite part of this job has been hosting a weekly coffee club where I organize a team of barista-engineers to showcase locally roasted single-origin coffees. I'm also a member of the company social committee where I've organized a high-ropes outing, a company dinner, an indoor put-put, a St. Patricks Day trivia, and other events during the pandemic.

I've been an embedded engineer for 2 years for Plexus (Raleigh) on a robotic surgeon prototype to compete with the DaVinci robot. I made a testing suite for it that formed a network between a hardware prototype, an electrical stimulator, and a Windows computer. 

Before I was an embedded engineer, I was a journalist for 2 years at the NC State technician and a bioinformatics research assistant at NC State's department of computer science. I managed months-long investigations into issues of equity, diversity, and accessibility and interviewed almost everyone in campus administration at least once. I was a private tutor in ECE subjects as well during this time as well as an ECE TA for 3 semesters.

extra_fields.Previous Involvement: I used to teach high schoolers maths and physics at an NGO in India
extra_fields.Why Interested: I have learnt a lot during my time from my peers and professors during my time at NCSU. I have grown in my skills and my understanding of the field through my professional experience and it is an honor to give back to my community that has once helped me in any way possible. I have interest in mentoring and sharing my knowledge and discussing the possibilities and advancement of my field and knowing their thoughts and opinions

records.about_yourself: I am a 27 yr old hardware enthusiast who has always been intrigued by the technology advancement and thirst to try new experiences and learn more. I like to sing Indian classical and paint at my free time. I love to hang out with friends and play games. Though I am a dog lover , I don’t own one. I have plans to own a dog in future when I am sure I can provide him/her with full attention of mine and good home
records.professional_experience: I have been working as Design verification engineer at Marvell since my internship in May 2022 and finally joined as full time in Jan 2023. Previously before my masters, I worked as integration engineer on cloud software at SAP

extra_fields.Previous Involvement: Goodnight scholars program
extra_fields.Why Interested: I would like to give back to the community that helped me grow.

records.about_yourself: I love playing sports whenever I get a chance. Currently, I am playing badminton and ping pong.
records.professional_experience: I am Analog Designer, previously worked at Renesas Electronics and currently working at Analog Devices. I have interest in Analog and Power Management IC.

extra_fields.Previous Involvement: I've been unofficially mentoring people interested in Computer Engineering ever since I was a Junior in undergrad. I was also a part of Maitri, NCSU's graduate Indian Association, through which I mentored more than a hundred incoming Indian students between 2019 and 2021. I was also previously involved with the alumni mentor system (dropped out in 2023 due to other commitments)
extra_fields.Why Interested: I received a lot of help from my seniors when I was lost and needed career and academic advice. I managed to create a robust social circle for myself so I didn't need to rely on official channels like the alumni mentors to get the advice that I needed, but I recognize that not everyone can do that and that people relying on this system are the ones who need advice the most, so I'd like to give back through this channel.

records.about_yourself: I like exploring new things and my hobbies are varied and keep changing. I read (huge fantasy geek), write stories and poems, cook interesting dishes, play video games with friends, play badminton, do archery occasionally, etc. and I'm currently trying my hand at playing the ukulele. I'm social and I believe in making people I'm communicating with feel comfortable and confident around me. I live alone in America (a few relatives scattered across the east coast), with most of my family being in India. Having gone through the full cycle of being an international student, I can empathize and understand the struggles that an international student can face in their academics and personal life here.
records.professional_experience: I'm specialize in embedded systems, and my current role in Qualcomm revolves around using my embedded systems skills to develop and test software for radio frequency boards in Qualcomm's 6G and satellite communication prototype boards. I've been in the R&D environment since undergrad, having interned in an Indian government lab during undergrad, worked as a student worker in NCSU's ASSIST research center during my masters, and now working in Qualcomm's Wireless R&D division after graduating from NCSU.

extra_fields.Why Interested: I want to see my juniors succeed in life!

records.professional_experience: My field of interest would be designing chips for computers and mobile phones

extra_fields.Previous Involvement: Mentoring experience as part of my current and previous job roles/employers
extra_fields.Why Interested: It's always nice to talk to new/recent college graduates. Also, this is one way of giving back to NC State and especially the ECE department.

records.about_yourself: Well, I currently live in Apex, with my wife and 2 young boys. In my free time, I enjoy spending time with my family, play soccer with the boys, listening to music, and oh, how could I forget -- my XBOX!
records.professional_experience: After graduating from State back in 2010, I got into Juniper Networks as a Verification engineer. In my 9+ year stint at Juniper, I was part of a team that successfully taped out multiple networking ASICs. Working with them gave me the experience and exposure I was looking for coming out of school. From ASIC Verification to ASIC design, to post silicon validation and bring up - I have been part of the entire ASIC design cycle. I joined Microsoft back in Fall of 2019 and currently part of this state of the art Hardware team - right here in the triangle :)

extra_fields.Previous Involvement: yes
extra_fields.Why Interested: My Masters curriculum at NC State imparted me with the knowledge which I use in my everyday work. When I was a student, I did not get a chance to have a mentor who is working in the industry. Now that ECE dept at NC state itself is presenting with such opportunity for students and with an intent to help a student or two with his/her grad journey or professional career, I want to be considered for mentorship.

records.industry_focus_area: Data science / analytics, Artificial intelligence / machine learning
records.about_yourself: I am from India specifically from southern part i.e. Bangalore, Karnataka. My family of 5 consists of wife, parents and brother. During my free time, I like to work on my fitness, playing cricket, badminton and FIFA. I actively follow cricket and soccer and a fan of Manchester United.
records.professional_experience: I started my professional journey with Oracle in techno-functional role though my Bachelors was in ECE. With an intent to learn more and work in Electrical field, I opted for MS in Computer Engineering at NC State. Post my Masters I started working at Qualcomm as SoC DV Engineer where I was invlolved with Bus team. I worked on different protocols like AHB, AXI and other Qualcomm specific protocols. After some time, I grew inclination towards RTL design and switch to Modem design team within Qualcomm. Currently, I work on designing the datapath for the control channel of the Downlink blocks within the team.

extra_fields.Why Interested: I was an RA for a couple years at State and I enjoyed being able to share my experience with my residents. I've had a few residents who were engineering majors (including a year with the Engineering Village in 2019-2020 that was cut short) and it was rewarding to help guide them through being an EFY. Beyond that, I feel that being a recent dual degree graduate I can provide a fresh perspective on the struggles of the ECE program and getting through the course work.

records.about_yourself:
Since 2013, I’ve been a big lacrosse fan but I haven’t played since 2018 besides a couple men’s league games. When I get the chance, I keep up with the sport at all levels; international, box lacrosse, college, and professional. It was my first passion and I still intend to play and coach in the future!
While I was at State, I was active with the Filipino American Student Association and am currently in a similar group at Cisco. I hope to continue supporting Asian and Filipino orgs in the RTP while I’m living here.
My other interests are cooking, small form factor (ITX) PC’s, sports, and Smash Ultimate. Since graduating, my girlfriend and I have had more time to try new recipes and so far my favorite has been a baked chicken katsu! I just think ITX computers being able to pack strong computing power in a small footprint is a neat thing. I try to keep up with other sports news, and I play Smash Ultimate casually in my spare time (although I’m not particularly good).
I hope I’ll be able to participate in this mentor program as a valuable resource for ECE students through this tough degree!
records.professional_experience: I have started working at Cisco as an entry level Software Engineer since graduating in May 2022. I am currently working remotely in Raleigh with a team developing network interfaces and features in the new 8000 series of routers that Cisco will be coming out with. Mainly my work so far has been fixing low level bugs, developing unit tests, running interface tests on simulators and hardware, and triage for internally reported bugs.
